title: Senate Defies Trump on Iran, Hormuz Evacuation Begins, and Europe Swelters
subhead: The Republican-controlled Senate passed a war powers resolution to halt the Iran conflict, the UN started evacuating 11,000 stranded sailors from Hormuz, and a record heatwave killed dozens in France — while Israel-Lebanon ceasefire frayed and US-Iran negotiators clashed over nuclear inspections.

sections.heading: Senate Votes to Restrain Trump on Iran War
sections.body:
The Republican-controlled US Senate passed a war powers resolution 50-48 on Tuesday, directing the president to end US military involvement in Iran unless Congress authorises it. The House had already passed the same measure earlier this month. The resolution is largely symbolic: it will not be sent to Trump's desk, and even if it were, a veto override is out of reach. But the vote marks the most significant congressional rebellion against the Iran war to date, with several Republicans breaking ranks to support it.

According to AP, the resolution underscores growing unease in Washington about the war's trajectory and cost. The Pentagon has separately requested roughly $80 billion to cover war costs, a figure that has drawn pushback from both parties. Democratic Senator Patty Murray told Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth that Congress was spending 'families' hard-earned tax dollars on a war that many strongly oppose.'
sections.whyItMatters: The vote won't stop the war, but it signals that congressional patience is eroding. If the 60-day negotiating window with Iran stalls or collapses, the resolution gives opponents a procedural foothold to force further votes on war funding and authorisation.

sections.heading: Hormuz Evacuation Begins as Shipping Slowly Recovers
sections.body:
The International Maritime Organization announced Tuesday that it has begun coordinating a large-scale evacuation of more than 11,000 seafarers stranded on ships in the Strait of Hormuz since Iran effectively closed the waterway in late February. IMO Secretary-General Arsenio Dominguez said the operation is being conducted with safety guarantees from Iran, Oman, the US, and all coastal states, and that ships will be moved gradually to avoid collision risks.

According to AP, the evacuation runs alongside a slow recovery in commercial traffic. Data firm Kpler confirmed 131 ships transited the strait between Friday and Monday, including 39 on Monday alone — still well below the pre-war daily average of 100-130 vessels. The main central route remains mined and closed; ships are using smaller northern and southern corridors through Iranian and Omani waters respectively.

The MoU between the US and Iran gives Iran interim management of the strait for 60 days while it negotiates with Oman and six Gulf states over future administration. Iran has agreed not to charge tolls during that period, but Trump suggested on Saturday that the US could impose its own tolls for 'services rendered as the Guardian Angel to the countries of the Middle East.' Legal experts and maritime associations have repeatedly warned that any toll regime would violate the UN Convention on the Law of the Sea's transit passage provisions.
sections.whyItMatters: The evacuation addresses a humanitarian crisis that has been building for four months. But the longer-term question — who controls Hormuz and whether tolls will become normalised — remains unresolved, and Trump's toll comments have introduced new uncertainty into the negotiating framework.

sections.heading: US and Iran Disagree on Nuclear Inspections
sections.body:
A dispute over nuclear inspections has clouded the second day of US-Iran negotiations in Switzerland. Vice President JD Vance said on Monday that Iran had agreed to allow UN inspections of its nuclear sites. Iran's Foreign Ministry spokesperson Esmail Baghaei rejected that claim outright on Tuesday, telling reporters in Tehran that UN inspectors were 'not scheduled' to examine sites bombed by the US during the war. Trump said that if Iran had not agreed to inspections, he would 'cut off talks immediately,' but added there was 'no rush' for inspections to begin.

According to AP, the IAEA has not responded to requests for comment on its possible role. The agency has been in and out of Iran since Israel's 12-day war in 2025, but has not been granted access to US-bombed enrichment sites. Iran maintains its nuclear programme is peaceful, though the IAEA has said Tehran possesses highly enriched uranium that could be weaponised. Meanwhile, Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian, visiting Islamabad, declared that Iran would 'never compromise or negotiate our missile capabilities,' saying the MoU signed with the US made no mention of the missile programme.
sections.whyItMatters: Nuclear inspections are the verification mechanism that determines whether any final deal has teeth. If Iran refuses IAEA access to bombed sites, there is no way to confirm enrichment has stopped — and Trump has made inspections a condition of continued talks. This disagreement is the first real test of whether the 60-day roadmap can produce results.

sections.heading: Lebanon Ceasefire Frays as Two Killed by Israeli Fire
sections.body:
Israeli soldiers shot and killed two people in southern Lebanon on Tuesday, the first fatal incident since a ceasefire between Israel and Hezbollah appeared to take hold over the weekend. Lebanon's health ministry identified the dead as Mohammed Amhaz and Sajed al-Hajj Ali, who were near a bulldozer clearing rubble in Nabatieh al-Fawqa. The Israeli military said soldiers fired at 'four Hezbollah terrorists riding a bulldozer and a motorcycle' who had entered Israel's self-declared 'security zone' and ignored warning shots.

According to BBC, the killings came as Lebanese and Israeli officials opened talks in Washington aimed at a 'comprehensive peace and security agreement.' Iran's ambassador to the UN in Geneva, Ali Bahreini, warned that 'Lebanon is an unquestionable part of the agreement, and whatever happens in Lebanon affects the whole process.' Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu stated Monday that Israeli troops have 'full freedom of action' against Hezbollah and will remain in Lebanon 'as long as necessary.'

At least 4,192 people have been killed in Israeli attacks in Lebanon since March, according to the Lebanese health ministry. The ceasefire has largely held since Sunday, but Tuesday's deaths show how fragile it remains.
sections.whyItMatters: Every violation in Lebanon risks pulling the US-Iran deal apart. Iran has made a Lebanon ceasefire a top condition; Israel refuses to withdraw. The Washington talks are the first direct channel, but Netanyahu's 'full freedom of action' declaration and Tuesday's killings suggest the gap between the two positions is still wide.

sections.heading: Record Heatwave Kills Dozens in Europe
sections.body:
France recorded its hottest day ever on Tuesday, with a national thermal indicator of 29.8°C, surpassing the previous records set during the deadly 2003 and 2019 heatwaves. More than half the country is under red alert. At least 40 people have drowned in France since Thursday, many of them young people seeking relief in rivers and canals, Prime Minister Sébastien Lecornu told reporters. Two children aged two and four were found dead in a car in Carpentras.

According to BBC and AP, the heatwave extends across the continent. Spain has issued red alerts for Andalusia and the Basque Country, with temperatures reaching 45°C in Andújar. Italy declared red heat alerts in 15 cities, including Rome, Milan, and Venice, with the government reviving emergency labour protections for outdoor workers. In Germany, six fatal swimming incidents were recorded over the weekend and temperatures are expected to hit 40°C by week's end. The Golfech nuclear power plant in southwestern France was shut down because River Garonne water temperatures exceeded the 28°C legal limit for reactor cooling.

The Louvre announced early closures through Saturday, citing a building 'not sufficiently adapted to climate change.' The Eiffel Tower closed at 16:00 instead of its usual midnight. Europe is the fastest-warming continent, heating at twice the global average, according to Copernicus. Over the past four years, more than 200,000 heat-related deaths have been recorded across Europe, the WHO's Europe office said this month.
sections.whyItMatters: This is not a one-off event. June heatwaves in Spain have gone from two in 25 years (1975-2000) to ten in 25 years (2000-2025). The infrastructure — nuclear plants, railways, cultural landmarks — is visibly not designed for these temperatures. The 2003 heatwave killed an estimated 15,000 in France; this one is hitting in June, not August, meaning the peak of summer is still ahead.

sections.heading: Keir Starmer Resigns as Brexit's Political Legacy Claims Its Seventh PM
sections.body:
UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer announced his resignation on Monday, making him Britain's seventh prime minister since the Brexit referendum a decade ago. According to AP, Starmer's departure after two years in office followed a sluggish economy, malfunctioning government, and a divided electorate — legacies, at least in part, of Brexit. He had promised a 'reset' with the EU but refused to consider rejoining the single market.

The Brexit referendum, held on 23 June 2016, fractured the Conservative Party, ejected David Cameron, Theresa May, Boris Johnson, and Liz Truss in rapid succession, and ultimately opened the door for Labour's 2024 victory. But Starmer's government struggled with many of the same structural problems: low growth, trade barriers with the EU, and an electorate that has increasingly deserted both major parties for the Greens and Reform UK. Nigel Farage's hard-right party now consistently leads opinion polls.

According to AP, historian Chris Grey said 'the subterranean trace of Brexit' still runs through British politics. Anthony Seldon noted that the referendum was supposed to settle the European question — instead, 'the people who obsessed about it still obsess about it. Britain's problems have continued.'
sections.whyItMatters: Seven prime ministers in ten years is not normal for a G7 democracy. Starmer's resignation shows that the political system Brexit broke has not been repaired. The UK is heading into another leadership contest with no party offering a clear path out of the economic and constitutional consequences of leaving the EU.

bottomLine: June 24 brought three developments pulling in different directions. The Senate's war powers vote is a political signal that support for the Iran war is waning in Washington, even if it won't change policy yet. The IMO evacuation of stranded sailors from Hormuz is a humanitarian step forward, but Trump's musings about US tolls on the strait inject fresh uncertainty into the diplomatic framework. And the dispute over nuclear inspections — Iran denies it agreed to them, Trump says they're a condition — is the first concrete test of whether the 60-day roadmap can survive contact with reality. Meanwhile, Lebanon's ceasefire is already bleeding, Europe is literally on fire, and Britain has lost its seventh prime minister in a decade. The deals and systems that were supposed to stabilise things are being tested from every direction.
